title: CORE TESTING PRINCIPLES & TEST DESIGN TECHNIQUES

questions.question: Which of the 7 Testing Principles is most frequently ignored in projects? What are the consequences?
questions.answer:
The most neglected principle is Principle 6: Testing is context-dependent. Teams routinely apply the same test strategy, scripts, and coverage metrics across all projects — regardless of domain risk, technology stack, or business impact. An e-commerce platform and a medical device system have radically different risk profiles and require completely different testing approaches.
Consequences: (1) Critical safety or security flows are undertested in high-risk domains. (2) Budget is wasted on exhaustive testing of trivial features while genuinely risky areas are skimmed. (3) Teams miss domain-specific failure modes — e.g., applying generic web-app patterns to a real-time financial transaction system.
A close second is Principle 3: Early testing saves time and money (Shift-Left). Despite industry research showing defects fixed in requirements phase cost 1x but the same defect in production costs 100x, most teams still wait until code is delivered before QC engages. The result: compressed test cycles, hotfix releases, and cascading technical debt.
The most MISUNDERSTOOD principle is Principle 1: Testing shows the presence of defects, not their absence. Stakeholders still interpret 'all tests passed' as 'no bugs exist.' A system that passes 500 test cases may still contain thousands of untested paths hiding defects. QC must proactively educate stakeholders on this distinction.

questions.question: When should you use EP, BVA, Decision Table, or State Transition Testing? Is there a single 'most important' technique?
questions.answer:
Equivalence Partitioning (EP): Use when input space can be grouped into classes the system treats identically. Instead of testing every value, pick one representative per valid and invalid partition. Example: a discount system treating ages 0-17, 18-64, and 65+ differently — test one value from each group.
Boundary Value Analysis (BVA): Always layer on top of EP. Test the boundary values between partitions where defects most commonly occur (e.g., 17, 18, 64, 65). EP defines WHICH partitions exist; BVA defines WHERE within those partitions to probe.
Decision Table Testing: Ideal when output depends on a combination of multiple input conditions. Example: insurance premium calculation based on age + smoking status + pre-existing conditions. Use when you need to cover all rule combinations systematically to avoid missing a critical business rule.
State Transition Testing: Models systems that behave differently depending on their current state — order management (Draft, Processing, Shipped, Delivered, Cancelled), ATMs, booking workflows, user account states. Tests valid transitions, invalid/forbidden transitions, and multi-step sequences.
There is no single 'most important' technique. EP + BVA is the everyday foundation for input validation. Decision Tables excel at business rule verification. State Transition is essential for workflow-driven systems. A senior tester selects and combines techniques based on what the module does and where the risk lies.

questions.question: Why is BVA necessary when EP already exists? Give an example of a bug only BVA would catch.
questions.answer:
EP and BVA serve different purposes. EP confirms the system handles different categories of input correctly. BVA confirms the system handles the transition BETWEEN categories correctly. Defects cluster at boundaries because developers make precision errors: they use > instead of >=, or type 17 instead of 18 when coding a limit.
Example: A loan application requires an applicant to be between 18 and 65 years old. EP partitions: invalid (under 18), valid (18-65), invalid (over 65). EP test cases: age = 10, age = 35, age = 70. All three pass cleanly even if the code has a bug.
The bug: the developer coded age > 18 instead of age >= 18, so 18-year-olds are incorrectly rejected. EP's representative value of 35 passes both conditions and never exposes this. BVA test case: age = 18 (should be VALID). This test FAILS and catches the bug immediately.
Another classic example: Free shipping threshold at exactly $100. EP: under $100 (no free shipping) and $100+ (free shipping). EP tests $50 and $150 — both pass. BVA tests $99 (no free shipping) and $100 (free shipping). If the code uses amount > 100 instead of amount >= 100, the $100 case incorrectly charges shipping — a defect only BVA exposes.

questions.question: How do you ensure complete State Transition Testing coverage in complex business flows?
questions.answer:
Step 1: Build a complete State Transition Diagram or Table. Map every state, every valid transition (event + guard condition), and every transition that should be INVALID. For an order system: Draft -> Submitted -> Processing -> Shipped -> Delivered, plus Cancel and Refund paths.
Step 2: Apply coverage criteria systematically. All-States Coverage: every state is visited at least once. All-Transitions Coverage: every valid transition is exercised at least once. Invalid Transitions: explicitly test paths that should be blocked (e.g., cannot jump from Draft directly to Shipped without going through Processing).
Step 3: For complex flows, use N-Switch Coverage — test all valid sequences of N consecutive transitions. For most business systems, 1-switch coverage (pairs of consecutive transitions) provides strong coverage against state-dependent bugs.
Step 4: Focus on edge cases: terminal states (Delivered, Cancelled — no further transitions expected), reversible transitions (can a Cancelled order be reactivated?), guard condition failures (transition Y requires Admin role — test as non-admin), and concurrent state changes (two users cancelling the same order simultaneously).
Step 5: Maintain a Transition Coverage Matrix — rows are 'from state', columns are 'to state'. Mark tested transitions. Unchecked cells with valid transitions are gaps that need test cases.

questions.question: What is Exploratory Testing? How do you test effectively when there is no specification?
questions.answer:
Exploratory Testing is the simultaneous process of learning about the system, designing tests, and executing them in real time. It is NOT random clicking. It is structured, risk-informed investigation driven by a tester's domain expertise, heuristics, and analytical curiosity — adapting continuously based on what the system reveals.
Without a spec, use testing heuristics — HICCUPPS: History (what bugs existed previously?), Image (what does the product claim to do?), Claims (what does the UI or documentation state?), Comparable Products (how do similar systems behave?), User Expectations (what would a typical user reasonably expect?), Purpose (what is the core value proposition?), Product (what properties should any software exhibit?).
Apply Session-Based Test Management (SBTM): define a Charter (the goal and scope), set a Timebox (60-90 minutes), execute, then debrief with session notes: areas covered, bugs found, open questions, and areas not yet explored.
Reverse-engineer requirements from the system's actual behavior. If a field accepts certain values, document what the system treats as valid. Consult developers, business analysts, support tickets, and user interviews. Use production logs or analytics to understand real user behavior patterns and focus testing on the most-traveled paths.

questions.question: When in the SDLC is Ad-hoc Testing ideal? Before or after Regression? Should you do it with incomplete specs?
questions.answer:
Ad-hoc Testing is most valuable immediately after the Smoke Test passes (build is stable) and before formal regression begins. It provides rapid, unscripted exploration that can surface showstopper bugs early, before expensive structured test execution wastes time on an unstable build.
With incomplete specs: YES — ad-hoc becomes MORE critical, not less. Scripted tests leave large gaps when requirements are vague. Ad-hoc exploration reveals what the system actually does, helping define implicit requirements from behavior, and quickly exposing misunderstood or missing functionality.
In a rushed project: Ad-hoc is the highest ROI activity because it finds critical bugs fastest. Apply structured charter-based ad-hoc rather than completely random exploration, directing effort at the highest-risk areas identified by the development team.
Sequencing: Run ad-hoc on new/changed features BEFORE regression to catch obvious blockers quickly. Run ad-hoc AFTER regression to explore areas the regression suite doesn't reach. Never substitute ad-hoc FOR regression — they serve fundamentally different purposes.

questions.question: How do you identify target areas in Ad-hoc Testing? Prioritize Happy Path or Edge Cases? How to apply Error Guessing?
questions.answer:
Target area identification: (1) Recently changed or new code — changes always break things. (2) Integration points — where two systems communicate, data contracts break. (3) Historically defect-prone modules — bugs cluster (Pareto principle; 80% of bugs in 20% of modules). (4) Complex business logic with multiple conditions. (5) Areas not yet covered by formal test cases. (6) Features written by less experienced developers.
Always test Happy Path first. A broken critical path is a release blocker — confirm the core workflow runs before exploring edges. Once the main flow is verified, systematically probe: empty/null inputs, maximum field lengths, special characters (apostrophes, angle brackets, null bytes), duplicate submissions, concurrent operations, permission boundary violations.
Error Guessing is experience-based hypothesis testing. Build a mental model of the implementation and guess where mistakes are likely: off-by-one boundary values, null/empty string handling, special characters breaking SQL or XML parsing, negative numbers in positive-only fields, strings longer than database column limits, timezone edge cases, floating-point precision in currency calculations, race conditions in concurrent flows.

questions.question: How do you ensure Reproducibility, Coverage, and Reporting in Ad-hoc Testing to convince management?
questions.answer:
Reproducibility: Record every significant finding immediately using screen recording tools (Loom, OBS). For each bug, document: exact step-by-step sequence, specific test data values, environment details (OS, browser, version), timestamp, and expected vs. actual behavior. Always verify reproducibility yourself before logging — an unverified report wastes developer time and destroys credibility.
Coverage: Use structured charter-based ad-hoc. Each charter defines scope (Module X), mission (Explore payment flow under network interruption), and timebox (90 minutes). After sessions, map charters to a risk coverage matrix showing which areas were explored and which remain. This converts 'random testing' into a defensible coverage artifact.
Reporting: Produce a Session Report after each session: time spent, scope explored, bugs found (with severity), follow-up questions, and unexplored risk areas. Aggregate into a weekly Ad-hoc Test Summary Report.
Presenting to management: Frame it as risk management. 'We completed 12 charter sessions covering 8 modules. Found 6 bugs (2 High). 3 high-risk areas remain unexplored — we need 2 more days to reduce that release risk.' This is far more compelling than 'we did some ad-hoc testing.'

questions.question: How do you understand and overcome the Pesticide Paradox, especially on long-running projects?
questions.answer:
The Pesticide Paradox: running the same tests repeatedly eventually stops finding new bugs. Surviving defects become 'resistant' — they live in untested paths. The tests have eliminated all easy-to-find bugs and now only verify what the team already knows works.
Overcoming on familiar modules: (1) Intentionally rotate testing techniques — if EP+BVA has been the standard, switch to exploratory charters or decision table walkthroughs. (2) Conduct periodic coverage gap analysis — identify which code paths, requirements, or user scenarios lack test coverage. (3) Bring fresh perspective — a team member who didn't write those tests explores differently. (4) Deliberately test what has NOT been tested, not what you know is already working.
For long-running projects: (1) Quarterly test case reviews — remove redundant tests, update outdated ones, add new ones for recent features. (2) Add regression tests for every production bug found — use defects as a map to untested paths. (3) Periodically rebuild the regression suite from current risk priorities rather than historical accumulation. (4) Track 'last bug found per module' — a module that hasn't yielded bugs in 3 consecutive cycles either needs deeper testing techniques or is genuinely low-risk.

questions.question: What does 'enough testing' mean? Which metrics do you use to confirm quality to management?
questions.answer:
Testing is never mathematically complete — the input space is infinite. 'Enough' is a risk-based decision: we have tested to the agreed exit criteria, and the residual risk is acceptable to the business. The business owns the go/no-go decision; QC provides the data to inform it.
Define exit criteria upfront in the Test Plan: (1) Minimum 95% of planned test cases executed. (2) Pass rate >= 98%. (3) Zero open Blocker or Critical defects. (4) All High severity defects either fixed or formally accepted as known risk with mitigation plan. (5) All P1 user stories verified against acceptance criteria.
Key metrics to present: (1) Test Execution Rate: planned vs. executed. (2) Pass Rate: percentage of tests passing. (3) Defect Density: bugs per module, identifying risky areas. (4) Defect Removal Efficiency (DRE): defects removed before release / total defects × 100% — target 95%+. (5) Requirements Coverage: % of requirements with at least one linked passing test. (6) Defect Resolution Rate: ratio of fixed bugs to total found.
Never just say 'testing is done.' Say: 'Per our agreed exit criteria, we achieved 96% test execution, 98.5% pass rate, zero Blocker defects, and DRE of 97.2%. Residual risk is LOW and within the business's accepted release threshold.'

questions.question: Differentiate Unit Test, Integration Test, and System Test.
questions.answer:
Unit Test: Tests the smallest independently testable component (a function, method, or class) in complete isolation. Written by developers, all dependencies are mocked or stubbed. Extremely fast, runs in milliseconds, executed on every commit in CI. Validates that a single piece of logic works correctly in isolation.
Integration Test: Tests the interaction between two or more components, modules, or services. Validates that units communicate correctly when connected — data flows between them, APIs honor their contracts, database queries return expected data. Slower than unit tests; may require real infrastructure or service stubs.
System Test: Tests the complete, fully integrated system against its requirements — both functional and non-functional. Conducted as black-box testing from the end-user perspective. All real components run together: UI, APIs, databases, third-party integrations, message queues. Slowest and most expensive; catches integration failures and environment-specific issues.
The Testing Pyramid: write many Unit tests (fast, cheap, isolated), fewer Integration tests (moderate cost, more realistic), and fewest System tests (slow, expensive, end-to-end). A common mistake is inverting this pyramid and writing mostly slow System tests, which creates expensive, brittle CI pipelines.

questions.question: Differentiate Smoke Test, Sanity Test, and Regression Test. When to reject a build on Smoke Test? Criteria for selecting a lean Regression suite?
questions.answer:
Smoke Test: A broad, shallow verification that a new build is stable enough to proceed to formal testing. Tests only the most critical paths: can you log in? Does the home page load? Does the database connect? Like checking if a car starts before attempting a test drive. Takes 15-30 minutes maximum.
Sanity Test: A narrow, deep check on a specific module or bug fix after a change. Verifies the specific fix works and didn't obviously break closely related functionality. Not a full regression — focused scope, fast execution. Used after a targeted patch or small change.
Regression Test: Comprehensive re-execution of the full test suite after any change to verify that existing, working functionality remains intact. Slower, covers broad scope, run before release to prevent defect leakage.
When to reject on Smoke Test: Reject immediately if any P1/Critical path is broken — login failure, main navigation broken, database connection errors, build startup crash, payment page doesn't render. Do not proceed with formal testing on an unstable build.
Lean Regression selection (Risk-Based): (1) Tests covering recently changed code and its dependencies. (2) Tests for historically defect-prone modules. (3) Tests covering critical user journeys (checkout, login, core business transactions). (4) Tests for integration points between major systems. (5) Tests that previously found real defects. Target 20-30% of full regression suite that covers 80% of risk.

questions.question: What is Test Shakeout? What is its role after deploying to staging?
questions.answer:
Test Shakeout (also called Build Shakeout or Shake Testing) is an informal, rapid verification performed immediately after deploying a new build to a target environment. It is less structured than Smoke Testing — it is about 'shaking' the deployment to see what immediately falls apart before any formal test execution begins.
Role after staging deployment: (1) Verify environment health — are all services running? Are database connections established? Are environment variables and configs correctly applied? (2) Quick walkthrough of 2-3 critical paths to confirm nothing is obviously broken at the infrastructure level. (3) Check application startup logs for errors, warnings, or missing configurations. (4) Verify third-party integrations are responsive (payment gateway, email service, external APIs). (5) Confirm the correct version/build was deployed (version number check).
Shakeout typically takes 15-30 minutes. If it reveals deployment failures (missing config, startup crash, missing DB migration), the build is returned to DevOps immediately — no test resources are wasted on a broken environment. Shakeout is the gate between 'deployment completed' and 'formal testing begins.'

questions.question: A feature passes Functional Testing but fails at System Testing. How do you investigate?
questions.answer:
The first step is to characterize the failure precisely: what error, in which scenario, under what conditions? System Test failures that don't appear in Functional Test almost always stem from differences in context — real integrations vs. mocks, real data volumes, concurrent users, or environment configuration.
Investigation angles: (1) Integration differences — in Functional Test, was a dependency mocked? At System Test, the real service is called. The mock may not accurately represent the real service's behavior (different response format, latency, error codes). (2) Data differences — Functional Test uses curated minimal data; System Test may run with more realistic volumes, revealing query performance issues or data validation logic that only triggers with specific data patterns.
Additional angles: (3) Environment configuration — different connection pool limits, memory constraints, timeout values between environments. (4) Concurrency — System Test may involve multiple simultaneous users, exposing race conditions invisible in isolated Functional Test. (5) End-to-end data flow — data transformations that look correct in individual units may produce unexpected results when chained through the full system.
Process: Use system-level logs to trace the failing request through every component. Compare environment configurations systematically. Identify the exact point of divergence — which component produces the first unexpected result. Reproduce the scenario in isolation to pinpoint root cause.

questions.question: Differentiate Performance Test, Stress Test, Load Test, Scalability Test, and Volume Test.
questions.answer:
Load Test: Applies the anticipated production workload (normal and peak user volumes) to verify the system meets its performance requirements. Goal: confirm the system is stable and within acceptable response times under expected conditions. Example: simulate 500 concurrent users for 1 hour.
Stress Test: Deliberately pushes the system beyond its stated capacity to find the breaking point and observe how it fails. Goal: understand failure modes, not just confirm normal operation. A system failing gracefully (returning informative errors) is better than crashing silently. Example: ramp up to 2000 users until the system collapses.
Performance Test: The broad umbrella category encompassing all testing focused on speed, stability, responsiveness, and scalability under load. Load and Stress tests are subsets of Performance testing.
Scalability Test: Verifies the system scales as expected when resources are added (horizontal: more servers, vertical: more CPU/RAM). Validates that adding resources results in proportional performance improvement. Goal: confirm the architecture supports growth.
Volume Test: Tests with very large amounts of DATA, not users. Focuses on database performance when handling millions of records — are queries still fast? Does export work with 10 million rows? Does the backup complete in time? Critical for data-intensive applications like financial systems and data warehouses.

questions.question: System handles 100-200 users fine but fails at 10% error rate with 300 users. What does '300' tell you? How do you find root cause?
questions.answer:
The 10% error rate at 300 users indicates a capacity boundary, NOT a complete break point. The system is degrading — some requests fail while others succeed — suggesting a bottleneck that is saturated at 300 users but not catastrophically overwhelmed. This is a warning zone, not a cliff edge.
What this means technically: A specific resource is exhausted at ~300 concurrent users. Common culprits: database connection pool (e.g., max_connections = 250), thread pool exhaustion in the application server, memory pressure causing garbage collection pauses, or a queue depth limit on a message broker.
Root cause investigation: (1) Identify WHICH requests fail — is it all API calls or specific endpoints? A specific endpoint failing suggests a bottleneck in that code path, not the entire system. (2) Monitor resource utilization at the point of failure: CPU, RAM, database connections, thread counts, file descriptors. (3) Check application logs for timeout exceptions, connection refused errors, or out-of-memory warnings. (4) Profile slow database queries — under high concurrency, N+1 query problems become catastrophic. (5) Check external dependency response times — is a downstream service slowing down under shared load?
Determine capacity limit: run load tests at 250, 275, 290, 300, 310 users to identify the precise degradation point. The true break point is where error rate exceeds your SLA threshold (typically 1-2%). 300 users with 10% errors is the approach zone, not the ceiling.

questions.question: How do you simulate millions of records for Data Load Testing without affecting a shared database?
questions.answer:
Core principle: Never run data volume tests against a shared database. Data load testing can corrupt production data, degrade performance for other teams, and is irreversible. Always use an isolated, dedicated database environment.
Synthetic data generation: Use tools like Faker.js (JavaScript), Python Faker, Mockaroo, or custom SQL generators to produce realistic but fake data at scale. Ensure generated data respects all business constraints: foreign key relationships, unique constraints, valid enum values, realistic distributions.
Efficient database seeding: Write parameterized SQL INSERT scripts or use database-specific bulk load tools (LOAD DATA INFILE in MySQL, COPY in PostgreSQL, BCP in SQL Server). These are 10-100x faster than individual inserts and can populate millions of records in minutes.
Environment management: Use a dedicated load test database provisioned from a schema clone (no production data). After testing, restore from the pre-test snapshot or simply drop and recreate the test database. For containerized environments, use Docker volumes that can be reset instantly.
For ongoing projects: Create and version-control data generation scripts in the repository. Team members can regenerate test data consistently. Consider using a data virtualization layer (Delphix, Actifio) for enterprise environments needing production-realistic data without actual PII exposure.

questions.question: Differentiate Alpha Testing and Beta Testing. How do you prepare data and environment?
questions.answer:
Alpha Testing: Conducted IN-HOUSE by internal QA teams, developers, and select internal users in a controlled, pre-release environment. Done BEFORE external release. The environment is a staging setup with full team access and monitoring. Goal: find and fix all remaining critical issues before any external exposure.
Beta Testing: Conducted by REAL END-USERS in their actual environment (or close to production) after Alpha is complete. A limited, controlled release to a subset of real users. The system is stable enough for external exposure, but not yet generally available. Goal: gather real-world feedback on usability, performance under real conditions, and edge cases the team didn't anticipate.
Alpha environment prep: Dedicated staging server mirroring production configuration. Curated test data covering all scenarios. Full logging and monitoring enabled. Test accounts with defined roles/permissions. External integrations may use sandbox/test credentials.
Beta environment prep: Production or near-production infrastructure. Real user data (or sanitized production data with proper masking). Real external service integrations. Monitoring and alerting active. Feedback collection mechanism (in-app forms, crash reporting, user interviews). Clear rollback plan if critical issues emerge. Selected beta users briefed on expectations and provided with support contact.

questions.question: UAT is done by the customer. What is the QC's role? How to handle a serious bug found in UAT that QC previously missed?
questions.answer:
QC's role in UAT: (1) Prepare and set up the UAT environment with correct configuration and test data. (2) Write or review UAT test scripts based on business acceptance scenarios. (3) Train and support end-users — answer questions about the system, help navigate the interface. (4) Triage and formally document defects found by users — ensure they are complete bug reports. (5) Track UAT progress against acceptance criteria. (6) Manage communication between business users and the development team. (7) Prepare the UAT sign-off documentation.
Handling serious bugs discovered in UAT that QC missed: (1) Immediate response: acknowledge the defect promptly, log it with complete details, and triage its severity. Never minimize or be defensive about the miss. (2) Impact assessment: determine if the defect blocks UAT completion or just requires scheduling a fix. (3) Communication: notify the PM and development lead immediately, with a realistic estimate for resolution.
Post-fix: (1) Retrospective: conduct a root cause analysis of the miss. Was this scenario in the test plan? If not, why not — was it unclear in requirements, or was it a known gap? If it was in the test plan, what happened during execution? (2) Prevention: add the missed scenario to the regression test suite immediately so it can never leak again. (3) Systemic improvement: review similar modules for analogous gaps. Never blame individuals — focus on process improvement.

questions.question: Shift-Left Testing: What does QC do in the Requirements phase to reduce bugs early? How do you convince stakeholders?
questions.answer:
In the Requirements phase, QC should: (1) Participate in requirement workshops, user story reviews, and design sessions — not as a passive observer but as an active challenger. (2) Review requirements for: ambiguity (undefined edge cases), contradictions (two requirements conflict), untestability (no measurable acceptance criteria), and missing scenarios (what happens when data is invalid?). (3) Write draft acceptance criteria alongside the BA to ensure every story is testable before development begins. (4) Identify missing edge cases: what happens when a required field is empty? When a date is in the past? When two users submit simultaneously?
Log 'requirement defects' — ambiguities and contradictions found in requirements are defects too, and fixing them before coding starts is the cheapest possible bug fix.
Convincing stakeholders: Use cost-of-quality data: IBM's Systems Sciences Institute research shows a defect found in requirements costs 1x to fix; the same defect found in production costs 100x. Even if stakeholders don't trust the exact ratio, the directional truth is undeniable. Offer to run a pilot: spend 2 hours in requirement review for one sprint, track how many issues were found and what would have happened if they'd reached development. The data from one sprint is more persuasive than any theoretical argument.

questions.question: Documentation is poorly written, unclear, or completely absent. How do you handle this without blocking progress?
questions.answer:
First action: Don't wait for perfect documentation. Start by identifying what IS known and begin exploratory testing on completed features while simultaneously pursuing clarification on unknowns.
Parallel activities: (1) Schedule a quick clarification meeting with the BA, PO, or developer — 30 minutes of conversation often replaces 5 hours of trying to interpret vague specs. (2) Document the decisions made in that meeting as informal test requirements so they become a shared artifact. (3) Use the system itself as documentation — test the existing behavior, reverse-engineer the expected behavior, and write acceptance tests based on what you observe.
Structured approach without specs: (1) Identify the business domain and research comparable products. (2) Build a rough feature list from UI elements, API endpoints, or stories. (3) Apply heuristics-based testing. (4) Maintain a living 'Test Findings Document' that captures both what was tested and what behavior was observed — this becomes de facto documentation.
Escalation: If ambiguity is causing meaningful test coverage gaps, flag it formally in the Test Plan risk section: 'Incomplete requirements for Module X — testing coverage may not be sufficient. Risk: defects may pass undetected.' This protects the QC team and formally transfers risk awareness to the PM/PO.

questions.question: How do you review an SRS document? What signals indicate an SRS is not ready for test case writing?
questions.answer:
SRS review criteria — a good SRS is: (1) Complete: all functional and non-functional requirements are defined; no 'TBD' placeholders in critical flows. (2) Correct: requirements accurately reflect the business need and are technically feasible. (3) Consistent: no contradictions between requirements. (4) Unambiguous: each requirement has exactly one possible interpretation. (5) Verifiable: you can write a measurable test that proves the requirement is met or not. (6) Traceable: requirements are uniquely identified and linked to business goals.
Red flags indicating SRS is NOT ready for test case writing: (1) Requirements using vague language: 'the system should respond quickly,' 'the UI should be user-friendly,' 'handle large amounts of data.' None of these are testable. (2) Contradictions: 'the user must be 18+ to register' and 'minors can register with parental consent' in different sections. (3) Missing edge cases: no definition of what happens with invalid input, timeouts, or network failures. (4) Circular references or forward references to undefined sections. (5) Missing non-functional requirements (performance thresholds, security requirements, browser support). (6) No acceptance criteria for user stories.
Action when SRS is insufficient: Log specific defects against each unclear/missing requirement, assign to the BA/PO with requested clarification, and include an SRS quality risk in the Test Plan. Do not begin writing formal test cases against an SRS that cannot support verifiable, reproducible test cases.

questions.question: What defines a high-quality Test Case? What do you check when reviewing a Fresher's test cases?
questions.answer:
A high-quality Test Case has: (1) Unique, descriptive ID and clear title stating what is being tested and what the expected outcome is. (2) Clear, complete, and reproducible preconditions — the exact state the system must be in before execution. (3) Specific test data — not 'enter some data' but 'enter first name = "John", last name = "O'Brien"'. (4) Step-by-step instructions precise enough for any team member to execute without interpretation. (5) Explicit expected result for EACH step, not just the final step. (6) Pass/Fail criteria that leave no room for subjective judgment. (7) Post-conditions — the system state after the test (especially for cleanup).
When reviewing a Fresher's test cases, check: (1) Are test cases testing ONE thing each, or are multiple behaviors crammed into one case? (2) Are expected results specific and measurable, or vague ('the system should work correctly')? (3) Is test data defined precisely, including boundary values and negative cases? (4) Do they have ONLY positive test cases, or do they cover negative scenarios (invalid data, unauthorized access, empty fields)? (5) Are preconditions complete — would another tester need to ask questions to set up the test? (6) Are there test cases for edge cases, not just the happy path? (7) Is every test case traceable to a requirement?
Common Fresher mistakes: writing 100 similar positive tests but no negatives; vague expected results; test data that only works on their specific account; missing cleanup/teardown; no test for empty or null inputs.

questions.question: Do you follow test cases 100%, or do you exercise judgment? When is it acceptable to deviate?
questions.answer:
Rigid 100% adherence to test cases is a sign of a junior tester, not a senior one. Test cases are the PLAN, not the law. A senior tester executes test cases while simultaneously observing the system for anomalies that fall outside the scripted steps.
Acceptable deviations: (1) During execution, you notice an anomaly adjacent to the test step being executed — note it, investigate it, don't ignore it because 'it wasn't in the test case.' (2) A requirement has changed since the test case was written and the case now tests the wrong behavior — update the case and follow the current requirement. (3) The expected result in the test case is itself wrong (contains a specification error) — flag it, correct it, then execute correctly. (4) A blocked prerequisite prevents certain steps from executing — document the blockage and skip gracefully, don't mark it Pass because you couldn't verify it.
When to strictly follow: Regulated environments (medical devices, avionics, financial compliance) often require documented, undeviated test execution with signature trails. Deviating without documented approval violates compliance. In these contexts, any deviation must be formally logged with justification and retested properly.
The principle: Follow the spirit of the test case (the business scenario being verified) over the letter (the exact words). Always document any deviation and its reason in the execution log.

questions.question: Why is API testing more important than UI testing, especially in Microservices architectures? What are the risks of testing UI only?
questions.answer:
In a Microservices architecture, the UI is merely a thin presentation layer. All actual business logic — validation rules, data transformations, authorization checks, workflow orchestration — lives in the APIs. Testing only the UI means you are testing through a translator that may hide what the API is actually doing.
APIs are consumed by many clients: web browsers, mobile apps, third-party partners, internal services. A UI test only covers one consumer. An API defect can affect all consumers simultaneously. If you only test the web UI, your mobile app, partner integrations, and internal service consumers remain untested at the business logic layer.
Risks of UI-only testing: (1) Business logic defects pass because the UI happens to display valid results by coincidence or caching. (2) Security vulnerabilities — authorization bypasses, IDOR attacks, parameter tampering — are invisible through UI because the browser hides raw requests. (3) Microservice contract breakages go undetected until a calling service fails at runtime. (4) Error handling quality cannot be observed — the UI often shows a generic 'Something went wrong' while the API returns exploitable stack traces.
API tests run faster, are less brittle than UI tests (no DOM selectors to break), and provide exact verification of business rules. They are the most efficient quality gate in a CI/CD pipeline.

questions.question: When should API testing begin? Should you test Syntax before Functional behavior?
questions.answer:
API testing should begin the moment a single endpoint is available — even before the UI exists. In Agile, this means QC starts API testing in the same sprint the developer implements the endpoint, not after UI integration is complete.
Test Syntax first, then Functional behavior. This is the correct order: (1) Syntax/Contract testing confirms the API returns the expected structure — correct field names, correct data types, required fields present, no extra undocumented fields. If the contract is wrong, functional tests will produce misleading results. (2) Functional testing then validates business logic — does a POST /orders create the correct order, trigger the right downstream events, and return the right status?
Practical workflow: Start with a Swagger/OpenAPI contract review. Verify the contract is accurate by running a few manual calls. Write schema validation assertions. Then build functional test scenarios (CRUD operations, authorization checks, error cases, boundary values). Finally add integration/chaining tests (create a resource, then retrieve it, then delete it, then verify it's gone).
Using Postman: create a Collection organized by endpoint. Write pre-request scripts for auth token management. Write test scripts for schema validation first, then add functional assertions. This ensures every functional test also implicitly validates the contract.

questions.question: Differentiate 401 vs 403 errors. What do you check in the HTTP Header?
questions.answer:
401 Unauthorized: The request lacks valid authentication credentials. The server does not know WHO you are. The client should re-authenticate. Common causes: missing Authorization header, expired JWT token, invalid API key, wrong token format.
403 Forbidden: The server knows exactly WHO you are (authentication succeeded) but you are NOT ALLOWED to perform this action. Authorization failure. Common causes: a regular user trying to access an admin endpoint, a user accessing another user's private data, insufficient role/permission for the requested resource.
Key distinction for testing: When you test a protected endpoint without a token, expect 401. When you test with a valid token for a user who lacks permission (e.g., a 'viewer' role trying to DELETE a resource), expect 403. Confusing these in implementation is a security defect.
Critical headers to check: (1) Authorization: Bearer [token] — is the token present, well-formed, and not expired? (2) Content-Type: application/json — correct for JSON bodies. (3) Accept: what format the client expects in response. (4) X-Request-ID or X-Correlation-ID — for traceability in microservices. (5) WWW-Authenticate — returned in 401 responses, tells the client how to authenticate. (6) CORS headers — Access-Control-Allow-Origin for browser clients. (7) X-RateLimit-Remaining — how many requests remain before rate limiting triggers.

questions.question: Differentiate Authentication vs Authorization. How do you test both for security?
questions.answer:
Authentication (AuthN): Verifies IDENTITY — 'Who are you?' The process of proving you are who you claim to be. Mechanisms: username/password, OAuth tokens, JWT, API keys, certificates, MFA. Authentication happens first.
Authorization (AuthZ): Verifies PERMISSION — 'What are you allowed to do?' After your identity is confirmed, the system checks if you have the right to perform the requested action on the requested resource. Role-Based Access Control (RBAC), attribute-based control, or policy-based systems enforce this.
Testing Authentication: (1) Test with no credentials — expect 401. (2) Test with invalid/expired credentials — expect 401. (3) Test with correct credentials — expect access. (4) Test token expiry — after expiry window, requests should fail. (5) Test token reuse after logout — invalidated token should return 401. (6) Test brute-force protection — after N failed attempts, account locks or CAPTCHA triggers.
Testing Authorization: (1) Horizontal privilege escalation — log in as User A and try to access User B's resources by modifying IDs in the request (IDOR). (2) Vertical privilege escalation — log in as a 'viewer' role and try admin endpoints (POST /admin/users, DELETE /admin/config). (3) Role boundary testing — each role should only access its permitted endpoints. (4) Unauthenticated access — call authenticated endpoints without any token. (5) Test that removing a permission immediately blocks access (no caching of old permissions).

questions.question: How do you handle HTTP 429 (Too Many Requests) in API testing?
questions.answer:
HTTP 429 Too Many Requests indicates the client has exceeded the rate limit set by the API. The server is protecting itself from abuse, overload, or denial-of-service. The response should include a Retry-After header specifying when the client may retry.
Testing rate limiting: (1) Send N+1 requests where N is the documented limit within the time window. The N+1 request should receive 429. (2) Verify the Retry-After header is present and contains a reasonable value. (3) After the retry window expires, confirm requests succeed again. (4) Test per-endpoint limits if documented differently from global limits. (5) Test per-user vs. per-IP limits — some APIs limit by authenticated user, others by IP address.
Tools for testing: In Postman, use the Collection Runner with a delay setting and run more iterations than the limit allows. Use Newman for command-line execution. For more sophisticated tests, use JMeter or k6 to send bursts of requests.
In automation handling 429: Implement exponential backoff — on receiving 429, wait for Retry-After duration (or use exponential backoff if no Retry-After), then retry. Log 429 responses distinctly from genuine failures so monitoring alerts don't trigger on expected rate-limit behavior during load tests.

questions.question: How do you test for IDOR (Insecure Direct Object Reference) vulnerabilities?
questions.answer:
IDOR is a vulnerability where an application uses a user-supplied identifier (like an ID in a URL) to directly access a database object without verifying that the requesting user has permission to access THAT specific object.
Basic IDOR test: (1) Log in as User A. Create a resource (order, profile, document). Note the resource ID in the response (e.g., /api/orders/1234). (2) Log in as User B (different account, same role). Attempt to access /api/orders/1234 directly. If User B can see or modify User A's order, IDOR is confirmed.
Advanced IDOR testing: (1) Try sequential IDs — if you own resource 1234, try 1233, 1235, 1000. Predictable IDs make IDOR trivially exploitable. (2) Test all HTTP methods — GET (read), PUT/PATCH (modify), DELETE — on another user's resource. (3) Test nested objects — /api/users/456/orders/1234 — can you access order 1234 by substituting the user ID? (4) Test with numeric, UUID, and alphanumeric IDs. (5) Test indirect references in request bodies, not just URLs.
Expected behavior: The API should return 403 Forbidden (not 404 or 200) when authenticated User B tries to access User A's private resource. Returning 404 is a security-through-obscurity approach; returning 403 is correct and explicit.

questions.question: Write a SQL query to get the Top 5 customers with the highest total order value in March 2026. Use JOIN + GROUP BY + HAVING.
questions.answer:
The query assumes two tables: customers (customer_id, customer_name) and orders (order_id, customer_id, total_amount, order_date, status).
SELECT c.customer_id, c.customer_name, SUM(o.total_amount) AS total_spent FROM customers c INNER JOIN orders o ON c.customer_id = o.customer_id WHERE o.order_date >= '2026-03-01' AND o.order_date < '2026-04-01' AND o.status != 'Cancelled' GROUP BY c.customer_id, c.customer_name HAVING SUM(o.total_amount) > 0 ORDER BY total_spent DESC LIMIT 5;
Explanation: INNER JOIN links customers to their orders. The WHERE clause filters to March 2026 using a date range (date range is more index-friendly than MONTH() function). HAVING SUM > 0 excludes customers with no qualifying orders. ORDER BY DESC puts highest total first. LIMIT 5 returns only the top 5.
Testing this query as QC: (1) Verify correct month filtering — seed test data with February 28, March 1, March 31, April 1 orders and confirm only March orders count. (2) Verify cancelled orders are excluded if that is a business rule. (3) Verify a customer with multiple orders gets their amounts summed correctly (not just counted). (4) Test the edge case where fewer than 5 customers exist — should return what is available, not an error. (5) Verify ties are handled consistently.

questions.question: How do you verify Data Integrity when INSERT/UPDATE operations touch multiple tables via Foreign Keys and Triggers?
questions.answer:
Data integrity across related tables requires testing at multiple levels. The goal is to verify that after any multi-table operation, the database remains in a consistent, valid state.
Foreign Key integrity tests: (1) INSERT a child record with a non-existent parent ID — expect a constraint violation error, not a silent insert. (2) DELETE a parent record that has child records — verify the configured behavior (CASCADE delete removes children, RESTRICT blocks the delete, SET NULL updates child references). (3) Verify that ON UPDATE CASCADE propagates parent ID changes to all children correctly.
Trigger testing: (1) Execute the triggering operation (INSERT/UPDATE/DELETE) and verify the trigger fires — check for side effects in audit tables, log tables, or computed fields. (2) Test trigger BEFORE vs AFTER behavior — for a BEFORE trigger that validates data, ensure invalid data is rejected before the main operation commits. (3) Test trigger error handling — if a trigger fails, does the entire transaction roll back? (4) Test for trigger chaining — does Trigger A's actions cause Trigger B to fire unexpectedly?
Transaction consistency: (1) Test that a multi-step operation (insert to orders + insert to order_items + update inventory) either ALL succeeds or ALL rolls back on failure. Inject a failure midway and verify no partial data is committed. (2) Verify row counts before and after operations. (3) Query the database directly to confirm what was persisted — never trust only the API response; the API may say 'success' while the transaction partially failed.

questions.question: What are the key differences between testing a Web Desktop application and a Mobile App?
questions.answer:
Browser vs. OS environment: Web tests must account for multiple browsers (Chrome, Firefox, Safari, Edge) and browser versions. Mobile tests must account for multiple OS versions (iOS 16/17/18, Android 12/13/14) and device manufacturers, each with potential behavior differences in rendering, permissions, and background processing.
Connectivity scenarios: Mobile requires explicit testing of network transitions — 4G to 3G, WiFi to cellular, and complete network loss. Web browsers rarely need this level of connectivity testing, and handling offline gracefully is much more critical on mobile.
Mobile-specific test types with no web equivalent: (1) Interruption testing — incoming calls, SMS, push notifications while the app is active. (2) Resource constraint testing — OS killing the app due to RAM pressure. (3) Battery and performance under low battery conditions. (4) Hardware integration — camera, GPS, accelerometer, biometric authentication. (5) App lifecycle — backgrounding, resuming, upgrade from previous version without data loss.
Gestures vs. mouse: Mobile uses tap, swipe, pinch, long press, double tap. Web uses click, hover, right-click, scroll. Touch targets must be minimum 44×44pt on mobile; hover states are irrelevant on touchscreens.
Distribution and updates: Mobile apps go through App Store/Google Play review processes. Web deployments are instant. This affects how quickly bugs can be patched after release — a mobile critical bug fix may take 24-48 hours to reach users through store review.

questions.question: How do you verify that a UI matches the design specification (HTML/CSS)?
questions.answer:
Primary tool: Chrome DevTools (or Firefox DevTools). Open DevTools, select the element with the inspector, and compare the computed CSS properties (font-family, font-size, color hex value, margin, padding, border-radius, line-height) against the Figma/Zeplin/design spec.
Systematic comparison: (1) Colors — use the DevTools color picker to extract the exact hex value and compare against the design system palette. (2) Typography — verify font-family, font-size (in px or rem), font-weight (400 vs 700 for bold), letter-spacing, line-height. (3) Spacing — inspect margin and padding in the computed styles panel; use the box model visualization. (4) Responsive behavior — use DevTools device emulation to check breakpoints defined in the spec.
Pixel-perfect tools: Browser extensions like PerfectPixel or PixelParallel overlay the design mockup as a semi-transparent layer over the rendered page, making visual discrepancies immediately visible.
Cross-browser verification: Check that the same styles render consistently across Chrome, Firefox, Safari, and Edge. CSS properties like `gap`, `aspect-ratio`, and some grid behaviors have inconsistencies across browsers. Run a quick visual comparison on each target browser.
Accessibility check: Verify that text meets WCAG contrast ratio requirements (4.5:1 for normal text, 3:1 for large text) using DevTools' accessibility panel or tools like axe DevTools.

questions.question: How do you determine if a slow web page is caused by a slow API or slow frontend rendering?
questions.answer:
Open Chrome DevTools → Network tab. Reload the page with the cache disabled (Ctrl+Shift+R or check 'Disable cache'). Observe the waterfall diagram.
Slow API diagnosis: Find the relevant API call in the Network tab. Check the Timing breakdown of that request: (1) TTFB (Time To First Byte) is the key metric — it represents pure server processing time. If TTFB is high (>500ms for a simple API), the backend is slow. (2) Look at 'Waiting (TTFB)' vs 'Content Download' in the request timing. High Waiting = slow server. High Content Download with low TTFB = large payload, possibly network or data size issue.
Slow frontend rendering diagnosis: If the API returns quickly (TTFB is fast, response received promptly) but the page still appears slow to become usable: (1) Check the Performance tab — record a session and look at Long Tasks (tasks blocking the main thread for >50ms), excessive JavaScript execution time, or layout thrashing. (2) Check for large JavaScript bundles in the Network tab (JS files over 500KB are a concern). (3) Observe Core Web Vitals: LCP (Largest Contentful Paint) — measures when the main content appears. High LCP with fast API = rendering bottleneck.
Conclusion to report: 'API response for /api/products returns in 120ms (TTFB: 95ms). However, the page takes 4.2 seconds to become interactive (TTI). The bottleneck is frontend — specifically, a 1.2MB JavaScript bundle blocks main thread rendering for 3.1 seconds. The backend is healthy; the issue is client-side.'

questions.question: Why does clearing session/cookie require a logout? What are the security risks?
questions.answer:
A session cookie contains a session token — a unique identifier that maps to the server-side session data (user identity, permissions, cart contents). When you delete this cookie without calling the logout endpoint, the cookie is gone from the browser BUT the server-side session record still exists. The session token remains valid on the server.
Security risk: Session Fixation and Session Hijacking. If an attacker captured the session token before you deleted it locally (via network interception, XSS, or shoulder surfing), they can still use that token to authenticate as you — even after you deleted your local cookie. The server has no way to know you intended to end the session.
Proper logout must: (1) Call the server-side logout endpoint which explicitly invalidates/deletes the session token from the server's session store (database, Redis, etc.). (2) Clear the session cookie from the browser. (3) Optionally redirect to the login page.
Test scenarios QC should cover: (1) Delete session cookie manually in DevTools → attempt to navigate to authenticated page → should redirect to login (because no token). (2) Capture session token → log out properly → try to reuse captured token in a new request → should receive 401. If step 2 returns anything other than 401, the logout is broken. (3) Session token should have a server-enforced expiry even without explicit logout. (4) Test concurrent sessions: if you log in on two browsers simultaneously and log out on one, does the other session remain valid (by design) or also terminate?

questions.question: How do you test an app being killed by the OS due to RAM or battery consumption?
questions.answer:
iOS testing: On iOS, the OS aggressively terminates background apps. Test by: (1) Open the app and navigate to a specific screen (e.g., filling out a form mid-entry). (2) Press Home to background the app. (3) Open multiple heavy apps (games, camera) to pressure memory. (4) Wait 5-10 minutes and return to your app. On a low-RAM device, iOS may have terminated it. (5) Verify: does the app return to the correct screen? Is form data preserved? Does it handle the cold restart gracefully without crashing?
Android testing: Use adb (Android Debug Bridge): 'adb shell am kill [package.name]' to simulate OS kill. Or use Developer Options → Running Services → manually force-stop the app. Then reopen and verify state restoration.
Battery simulation: On Android Developer Options, enable 'Battery saver' mode and run the app. On iOS, enable Low Power Mode. Verify: (1) App continues to function (possibly with reduced background activities). (2) Background refresh and push notifications are properly handled. (3) App correctly detects Low Power Mode and adjusts behavior as specified.
Expected behaviors to verify: (1) App state is saved to persistent storage before suspension. (2) On resume after OS kill, app presents either fresh state or correctly restored state (per spec). (3) No crash on resume. (4) No data loss for user input that was in progress. (5) Network reconnection is handled gracefully after resume.

questions.question: For a cross-platform app, what do you check to ensure consistent UI/UX between iOS and Android?
questions.answer:
Visual consistency: Run the same screens side-by-side on both platforms. Check: (1) Typography — font rendering differs between iOS (San Francisco) and Android (Roboto) even for the same font-size setting. Ensure text doesn't overflow containers on either platform. (2) Colors — color rendering can differ between display technologies (OLED on Android Pixels vs. LCD on iPhones). (3) Icon rendering — SF Symbols on iOS vs. Material Icons on Android; verify design intent is preserved even if icon sets differ.
Layout and spacing: Viewport safe areas differ significantly. iOS has the notch/Dynamic Island and home indicator. Android has navigation bars (gesture or button) and various notch styles. Verify the app correctly uses Safe Area insets on iOS and handles Android's navigation bar height dynamically. Check landscape orientation if supported.
Platform-specific behaviors: (1) Back navigation — Android has a system back button/gesture; iOS uses the swipe-from-left edge or in-app back buttons. Test both navigation patterns. (2) Keyboard behavior — iOS and Android keyboards push content up differently. Verify forms remain accessible when the keyboard appears. (3) Permissions dialogs — different UI and grant/deny patterns between platforms. (4) Date/time pickers — default UI components look completely different; verify both are functional.
Functional consistency: The same user action should produce the same business result on both platforms. Test the core user journey (register, complete purchase, submit form) on both platforms with the same data. Use device farms (BrowserStack, Firebase Test Lab) to extend coverage across device models.

questions.question: How do you perform Interruption Testing (calls, SMS, low battery, network loss)?
questions.answer:
Phone call interruption: (1) Start using the app actively (mid-transaction, video playing, form being filled). (2) Use a second phone to call the test device. (3) Observe: does the app pause gracefully when the call UI appears? (4) Accept the call, talk for 30 seconds, end the call. (5) Return to the app: verify it resumes exactly where it was, no data loss, audio state correctly restored, no crash.
SMS/notification interruption: Trigger a notification while the app is in foreground. Verify: (1) App banner notifications display over the app without breaking the current state. (2) Tapping the notification navigates to the correct in-app destination. (3) Dismissing the notification returns to the exact previous app state. (4) Test multiple rapid notifications (notification storm).
Low battery: Enable battery saver mode during active use. Verify: (1) App detects low battery state if applicable. (2) Background tasks are reduced appropriately. (3) App does not drain battery excessively even in background.
Network loss: (1) Disable network during an active operation (mid-API call, file upload). (2) Verify: appropriate error message shown (not crash), retry option available if applicable, offline mode activated if the app supports it, no data corruption from partial write. (3) Restore network. Verify: app reconnects gracefully, pending operations resume or prompt user to retry, no duplicate submissions occur on retry.

questions.question: How do you perform compatibility testing across thousands of Android/iOS devices without having all physical devices?
questions.answer:
Device farms and cloud testing platforms are the practical solution: (1) BrowserStack — largest device cloud; real physical devices, supports both manual and automated testing, iOS and Android. (2) Firebase Test Lab (Google) — real devices and emulators; deep integration with Android, free tier available. (3) AWS Device Farm — enterprise-scale; real devices, suitable for automation at scale. (4) Sauce Labs — supports mobile, web, and cross-platform testing.
Prioritization strategy: Test on ALL devices is neither feasible nor valuable. Use a risk-based device matrix: (1) Identify your actual user base using analytics (which OS versions, which device models account for 80% of your users?). (2) Prioritize the top 5-10 device/OS combinations that represent your real audience. (3) Include at least one low-end device (entry-level Android with limited RAM) and one high-end device per platform. (4) Cover the latest two major OS versions for each platform.
Emulators/simulators for development: Use emulators for fast feedback during development and basic functional verification. Reserve real-device testing for final validation, performance testing, hardware integration (camera, GPS, NFC), and OS-specific behavior verification.
Automated screenshot testing: Tools like Applitools Eyes or Percy can run automated visual regression tests across a matrix of device/OS combinations, flagging rendering differences automatically.

questions.question: When should you NOT automate tests?
questions.answer:
Do NOT automate when: (1) The feature is likely to change significantly in the near term. Automating a UI that is redesigned next sprint means the automation investment is immediately discarded. (2) The test is executed only once or very rarely. Automation ROI requires sufficient runs to recoup the writing investment. (3) The test requires human judgment that cannot be codified — subjective UX quality, visual design review, usability assessment.
Also avoid automating: (4) Exploratory testing scenarios. The value of exploratory testing comes from the human tester's adaptive, creative investigation. Scripting it destroys that value. (5) Tests that are faster to execute manually. A 2-minute manual test requiring 6 hours to automate has a break-even point of 180 runs — probably never reached for rare scenarios. (6) Highly volatile requirements — if the spec changes every sprint, automation becomes a maintenance burden rather than an asset.
Other no-automation scenarios: (7) One-time data migration verification — test it once manually with proper SQL queries, log the results, done. (8) Hardware/physical interaction that cannot be simulated (e.g., testing swipe gesture precision on specific physical devices). (9) Third-party UI integrations where you don't control the DOM structure — these break constantly.
The guiding principle: Automate what is stable, repetitive, and risk-critical. Keep manual what requires judgment, creativity, or is too volatile. A test suite of 500 solid automated tests beats 2000 fragile ones that require constant maintenance.

questions.question: What is the process for validating that automation scripts run correctly? Who reviews your automation code?
questions.answer:
Validation process: (1) Initial validation — run the script against a known-good build and verify it passes. Then introduce a known defect (or use a build where a bug exists) and verify the script FAILS. A test that never fails is not testing anything. (2) False positive check — run the test on a broken build and confirm it doesn't pass when it shouldn't. (3) Flakiness check — run the script 10+ times in sequence to detect intermittent failures caused by timing issues, race conditions, or environment instability.
Code review: Automation code should go through the same code review process as production code. A peer tester or developer reviews: correctness (does the test actually verify what it claims to?), maintainability (are locators stable? Are waits appropriate?), test independence (does this test affect other tests' state?), and assertion quality (are assertions specific enough to catch real defects?).
CI integration validation: After merging, verify the script runs reliably in the CI environment (different OS, different browser version, headless mode). CI environment differences can cause scripts to fail that pass locally.
Documentation: Each automation script should have a clear description of what scenario it covers and which requirement it traces to. This allows the team to understand coverage gaps and identify which tests to investigate when they fail in CI.

questions.question: How do you handle CAPTCHA in automation testing (technical and process perspectives)?
questions.answer:
Technical approaches: (1) Bypass via test environment configuration — the most practical and recommended approach. Work with developers to disable CAPTCHA in the test environment, or create a test-mode CAPTCHA that always passes with a specific token/key. This is clean, reliable, and doesn't involve any circumvention.
(2) Use third-party CAPTCHA solving services (2Captcha, Anti-Captcha, DeathByCaptcha) — these send the CAPTCHA image to human solvers who return the answer in seconds. Use in controlled, authorized testing contexts only. Never use against production without explicit written authorization. (3) For reCAPTCHA v3 (score-based, invisible) — use test keys provided by Google specifically for automation testing.
Process/workaround approach: (4) Test CAPTCHA in isolation — manually verify the CAPTCHA integration works (correct keys, challenge appears, solution is validated). Then automate everything EXCEPT the CAPTCHA step. Have a separate manual test that covers the CAPTCHA-protected flow. (5) Use mocking — mock the CAPTCHA verification service endpoint so that in testing, all CAPTCHA challenges auto-pass.
What to avoid: Never use automation to circumvent production CAPTCHA protection. This is: (a) a violation of the site's terms of service, (b) potentially illegal under computer fraud laws, and (c) defeats the security mechanism the team explicitly implemented. For production CAPTCHA, always use manual testing or the test keys approach.

questions.question: Differentiate Severity and Priority with real-world examples.
questions.answer:
Severity: The technical impact of the bug on the system. How badly does this break the software? Defined by QC/technical team based on functionality impact.
Priority: The urgency of fixing the bug based on business context. How urgently must this be fixed? Defined by the business/PM based on customer impact, release timeline, and business risk.
High Severity + High Priority: Login page crashes on submit → blocks all users from accessing the system. Fix immediately.
High Severity + Low Priority: A critical calculation error in the Year-End Financial Report feature, but the report is only run once a year and next run is 11 months away. Technically serious; business urgency is low right now.
Low Severity + High Priority: The company CEO's name is misspelled in the About Us page of an app launching tomorrow. Functionally trivial — the app still works perfectly. But the business visibility and embarrassment make this urgent to fix before launch.
Low Severity + Low Priority: A minor UI misalignment — a button is 3 pixels off from the design spec — on an internal admin tool used by 2 people. Fix in a future cleanup sprint.
Key takeaway: Severity is technical; Priority is business. They are independent dimensions and should never be conflated. QC assesses severity; the PM/business decides priority.

questions.question: A developer rejects your bug saying 'Cannot Reproduce' or 'It's a Feature.' How do you handle this without creating conflict?
questions.answer:
For 'Cannot Reproduce': (1) First, verify your own reproduction steps are air-tight — re-read them critically. Can you reproduce the bug NOW with your documented steps? If yes, the steps may be missing a critical detail (specific test data, account state, precise sequence). (2) Provide enhanced evidence: screen recording of the reproduction, browser/device details, exact test data used, network logs or console errors. (3) Offer to pair with the developer — sit together and reproduce in person. Most 'cannot reproduce' bugs are resolved in 10 minutes of pairing because the developer was testing with different data or environment.
For 'It's a Feature': (1) Go back to the source of truth — the requirement document, user story, or acceptance criteria. If the spec says X and the system does Y, that is a defect regardless of developer intent. Cite the specific requirement. (2) If the spec is ambiguous or silent on this behavior, schedule a 3-way conversation: QC + Developer + BA/PO. Let the business decide the intended behavior. (3) If the business agrees it is intentional behavior, create a 'Requirement Change' ticket to update the spec and close the bug as 'By Design' with documented rationale.
Communication style: Never say 'you're wrong.' Say: 'Here's the specific acceptance criterion (Section 3.2) that this contradicts. Can we look at this together?' Frame it as clarifying requirements, not assigning blame. The goal is quality, not winning arguments.

questions.question: How do you find the root cause of a difficult bug?
questions.answer:
Step 1 — Characterize precisely: what is the exact behavior? What is expected? Under what exact conditions (data, environment, user role, sequence of steps) does it occur? Build the smallest, most reliable reproduction case.
Step 2 — Isolate variables: change ONE thing at a time. Does the bug occur with different test data? Different user accounts? Different browsers? Different environments? Narrowing the reproducible conditions reveals which component owns the bug.
Step 3 — Trace the execution path: (1) Check browser DevTools Network tab — does the request look correct? What does the response contain? (2) Check server logs at the time of the failure — is there an exception stack trace? (3) Check the database — was the data written correctly? Is there corrupted/unexpected data upstream?
Step 4 — Apply the 5 Whys technique: ask 'why did this happen?' and keep asking why for each answer until you reach a root cause (a design decision, a missing validation, a race condition, a wrong assumption). Example: Bug: wrong order total. Why? Tax was not applied. Why? Tax calculation service returned null. Why? The service was not passed the correct region code. Why? The checkout flow doesn't capture region before calling tax service. Root cause: missing data capture step in checkout flow design.
Step 5 — Document the root cause in the bug report so the developer's fix addresses the actual cause, not just the symptom.

questions.question: A bug exists only in Production but cannot be reproduced in Staging or UAT. How do you investigate? What do you suspect first?
questions.answer:
Production-only bugs are among the most challenging. The first suspects, in priority order: (1) Data differences — Production has real user data including edge cases that test environments never have. A specific combination of user data, account history, or database state triggers the bug. Request an anonymized data snapshot from production for investigation. (2) Configuration differences — environment variables, feature flags, API keys, connection strings, CDN settings, HTTPS vs HTTP, third-party service sandbox vs live credentials. Compare configurations line by line between production and staging.
Further suspects: (3) Scale differences — production handles real traffic; staging is lightly loaded. The bug may be a race condition, deadlock, or concurrency issue that only appears under real concurrent usage. (4) Caching differences — production uses CDN caching, reverse proxy caching, and database query caches at scale. Staging may not have the same cache layer. Stale cache may serve incorrect data. (5) Third-party service differences — payment gateways, external APIs, and email services behave differently in sandbox vs. production mode.
Investigation process: (1) Collect production logs from the exact time the bug occurred — look for errors, warnings, and the request/response chain. (2) Compare production and staging environment configurations systematically. (3) Interview the affected user for exact reproduction steps and their account state. (4) Use production read-only database access (if available and authorized) to examine the specific data that triggered the bug. (5) If possible, deploy the same code to a production-like environment with production data to reproduce.
Prevention: Reduce environment gaps. Use Infrastructure-as-Code (Terraform, Ansible) to ensure staging configuration matches production. Use anonymized production data refreshes for staging regularly.

questions.question: What is the QC's role in Sprint Planning, Daily Standup, Sprint Review, Retrospective, and Backlog Refinement?
questions.answer:
Sprint Planning: QC contributes by: (1) Reviewing user stories for testability and clear acceptance criteria before they are committed to the sprint. (2) Estimating QC effort (test design, execution, regression) and raising concerns if stories lack detail for effective testing. (3) Flagging dependency risks — 'This feature requires Module Y to be complete first; make sure it's in scope.'
Daily Standup: QC reports: (1) What was tested yesterday, what was found. (2) What will be tested today. (3) Blockers — builds not available, environments down, test data missing, waiting for clarification. Also listens for developer completions that need to enter the QC queue.
Sprint Review: QC role is critical. Demo readiness verification — ensure the demo will run against a clean, working build. Participate in the demo by verifying acceptance criteria are met. Present test coverage metrics and open defect status to stakeholders.
Retrospective: QC contributes: (1) What worked well in the QC process this sprint? (2) What slowed down testing? (3) Process improvement suggestions — 'Developers completing stories without notifying QC created a bottleneck on day 8. Can we agree on a Definition of Done step that includes notifying QC?'
Backlog Refinement: QC's most strategically valuable ceremony. (1) Review upcoming stories for ambiguity, missing acceptance criteria, and untestable requirements. (2) Suggest splitting large stories that are too big to test in one sprint. (3) Identify shared test data needs across multiple stories. (4) Estimate testing complexity early when information is still fresh.

questions.question: How do you estimate test effort when specifications are sparse? How many test cases can you execute per day?
questions.answer:
Estimation factors when specs are sparse: (1) Feature complexity — how many business rules, conditions, and user flows are involved? (2) Risk level — higher-risk features (payments, authentication, data privacy) require deeper testing. (3) Dependency count — how many integrations and external dependencies need coordination? (4) Test data complexity — how much setup is needed? (5) Automation availability — is there existing automation that can be reused?
Techniques for sparse-spec estimation: (1) Three-point estimation: Best Case + Most Likely Case + Worst Case / 3. Factor in ambiguity as risk overhead (typically 20-30% buffer). (2) Analogy-based estimation: compare to a similar feature tested previously and adjust for differences. (3) Break down to sub-tasks: test design hours + test execution hours + defect management hours + regression hours. Sum these.
Test case execution rate: This varies enormously by complexity. Simple UI validation test cases: 20-30 per day. Medium-complexity functional tests with data setup: 10-15 per day. Complex API/integration tests with environment coordination: 5-10 per day. When in doubt, use historical velocity from previous sprints as your baseline.
Never commit to execution counts as the primary metric. Commit to scenarios covered and risk areas addressed. A tester who executes 30 superficial test cases per day provides less value than one who executes 10 deep, targeted test cases covering genuine business risk.

questions.question: You need 6 test devices but only have 3. How do you prioritize by risk?
questions.answer:
Step 1: Identify the device coverage matrix needed. List all device/OS combinations required: e.g., iOS 17 (iPhone 15), iOS 16 (iPhone 12), Android 14 (Samsung), Android 13 (Pixel), Android 12 (budget device), Android 11 (older support).
Step 2: Apply risk-based prioritization using two axes: (1) Market share — which device/OS combination represents the largest percentage of your actual user base? Analytics data or regional market research provides this. (2) Risk impact — are there known platform-specific issues? Is there a new OS version with breaking API changes? Is your payment flow device-specific?
Prioritization result: Keep the 3 highest-priority combinations on real devices. For example: most-used iOS version + most-used Android version + the budget Android (highest error rate historically). For lower-priority combinations: (1) Use cloud device farms (BrowserStack free tier, Firebase Test Lab) to run automated smoke tests remotely. (2) Use emulators/simulators for functional verification of stable features. (3) Schedule physical device sharing by time-block if other teams have the needed devices.
Document the coverage gap formally in the Test Plan: 'Due to device constraints, Android 11 and Android 12 will be tested only on emulators. Risk: device-specific hardware behavior untested on these versions.' This transfers risk awareness to the PM for an informed release decision.

questions.question: Urgent project handover — how do you ensure Knowledge Transfer is not lost?
questions.answer:
Mandatory documents that MUST exist before handover: (1) Test Plan — strategy, scope, approach, tools, environments, exit criteria. (2) Test Case Suite — complete, executed, with pass/fail results and notes. (3) Bug Report Archive — all defects with status, resolution, and workaround notes. (4) Environment Setup Guide — step-by-step guide to configure test environments, credentials, and test data. (5) Test Data Guide — what data exists, where, how to recreate it. (6) Known Issues Register — bugs that are open by design, deferred to next release, or environment-specific.
Process for urgent KT: (1) Pair for minimum 3 days: walk through the system together, with the incoming person executing real tests while you observe and coach. (2) Record video walkthroughs of complex flows — a screen recording of you walking through the system is more valuable than 50 pages of documentation. (3) Create a 'Top 10 Risk Areas' document — the highest-risk parts of the system that need the most attention. (4) Introduce the incoming tester to the key developers, PO, and BA they will need to contact.
After handover, remain available for questions for at least 1 sprint. The incoming tester will hit knowledge gaps that can't all be anticipated upfront. A quick 10-minute daily check-in during the transition sprint prevents critical blockers.

questions.question: Describe a process bottleneck you encountered and how you improved QC-Dev collaboration to reduce fix time.
questions.answer:
Common bottleneck: Developers completing features at end of sprint → batch of code arrives at QC simultaneously → QC queue backs up → testing compressed into last 2 days → bugs found too late to fix → stories carry over to next sprint.
Root cause: Lack of continuous handoff. The fix: implement a Continuous Testing Flow within the sprint. Developers move a story to 'Ready for QC' the moment it's functionally complete, not at sprint end. QC picks up stories continuously throughout the sprint, not all at once on day 8.
Supporting changes: (1) Definition of Done includes: 'Developer notifies QC in Jira/Slack when a story is ready for testing.' (2) QC maintains a testing WIP limit — no more than 2-3 stories in testing simultaneously, creating a pull-based flow. (3) Bug fix prioritization: bugs found in the current sprint get next-day fix commitment from the dev who owns the story. (4) Daily 10-minute QC-Dev sync: QC shares what is in testing and what blockers exist; developers share what is coming next.
